content: Skill development follows a predictable emotional arc: from fear, to familiar, to fluent, to fun. This progression requires doing things badly before doing them well, and framing early failure as part of the process rather than evidence of inadequacy.
original_quote: Skills require a progression — from fear, to familiar, to fluent, to fun. They require reps. They require doing it badly before you do it well.
implication: When onboarding people to new capabilities like AI, designers of learning experiences should explicitly normalize the early uncomfortable stages and build in low-stakes practice opportunities rather than expecting immediate competence.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: People are already experts in foundational digital tools — email, spreadsheets, the internet — without thinking of themselves that way, because the competence was built so gradually it became invisible. AI is the fourth foundational utility of the modern knowledge worker, alongside electricity, devices, and the internet.
original_quote: AI is the fourth foundational utility of the modern knowledge worker, alongside electricity, devices, and internet. Saying 'I don't need to become an AI expert' is the same as saying 'I don't need to become an internet expert.' It reveals a belief that AI isn't indispensable. It is.
implication: Framing AI as optional expertise misreads its trajectory. Leaders should treat AI fluency the way they treat digital literacy — not as a specialization, but as a baseline professional competency expected of everyone.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Well-meaning warnings that 'you can't become an AI expert in an afternoon' inadvertently give people permission to disengage entirely. The message people actually hear is that AI expertise is a separate professional identity they don't need to adopt.
original_quote: You could see it happen in real time. A kind of permission to disengage rippled through the crowd. I'm a hospitality expert. I'm a transportation expert. I'm an oil and gas expert. I don't need to become an AI expert.
implication: How AI is introduced in rooms of skeptical professionals matters enormously. Framing that emphasizes the difficulty or specialization of AI mastery can backfire, creating disengagement rather than motivation.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Albert Bandura's guided mastery research shows that small sequential wins don't just reduce fear of a specific task — they generalize. Tackling one feared challenge with graduated support increases self-efficacy broadly, causing people to stop seeing new challenges as threats entirely.
original_quote: Small, sequential wins don't just reduce fear of a specific thing — they generalize. Subjects who tackled one feared challenge with graduated support didn't just lose that one fear. They stopped seeing new challenges as threats entirely. Self-efficacy — your belief in your ability to handle difficulty — transfers.
implication: When helping people build AI skills, the goal of early exercises isn't to teach specific techniques — it's to generate self-efficacy. Each small win expands the learner's belief in what's possible, which is more valuable than any individual skill learned.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Utley reframed his daughter's bike-riding goal from 'learn to ride' to 'fall as many times as possible.' This broke the tension and fear, and she began succeeding sooner than she would have otherwise. The same reframe applies to AI learning: your job is to prompt badly and iterate, not to get it right.
original_quote: I reframed the goal. Your job today is to fall. That's it. See how many times you can fall. She started laughing. The tension broke. And she got on the bike in a completely different spirit — and started succeeding far sooner than she would have otherwise.
implication: Facilitators and managers introducing AI should explicitly make failure the metric of early success. Redefining 'progress' as number of attempts rather than quality of outputs removes shame and accelerates genuine learning.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: According to Brice Challamel (former Head of AI at Moderna, now at OpenAI), the critical inflection point in AI capability isn't between advanced users and daily practitioners — it's between occasional users and daily users. Daily practice is what creates momentum and compounds gains.
original_quote: His observation: the critical inflection isn't between buckets 3 and 4. It's between 2 and 3. Daily practice is what creates momentum.
implication: Organizations should measure and incentivize daily AI usage across all employees rather than focusing resources on developing a small cohort of advanced power users. Consistent daily engagement is the lever that matters most.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Challamel's analogy — 'using AI is like showering, if you don't do it every day it's a problem' — reframes AI engagement as basic professional hygiene rather than advanced capability. It shifts the conversation from 'how much' to 'how consistently.'
original_quote: Using AI is like showering. If you don't do it every day, it's a problem — and at some point, there are diminishing returns.
implication: Leaders building AI culture should communicate a frequency expectation, not just a capability expectation. The standard isn't 'become an expert' — it's 'engage every day,' which is both more achievable and more impactful.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Brad Anderson, president of Qualtrics, argues that companies wanting to lead in the AI age need every employee using AI every single day — every employee, every function, every day. This reframes AI adoption as a universal organizational discipline, not a function of specific roles.
original_quote: If a company wants to lead in the AI age, every employee has to use AI every single day. Every employee. Every function. Every day.
implication: AI strategy should not be confined to technical teams or innovation labs. Executives should set explicit universal daily-use expectations and build the cultural infrastructure to support them across all functions.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: James Clear's research finding that 1% daily improvement compounds to 37x over a year is the mathematical explanation for why daily AI practice creates such dramatic capability gaps. The gap between occasional and daily users isn't talent — it's accumulated reps.
original_quote: James Clear cites research showing that 1% daily improvement compounds to 37x over a year. (1% daily decline? You lose 97% of your capability.) That's the math behind what Brice is observing — the gap between bucket 2 and bucket 3 isn't talent or technical aptitude. It's daily reps.
implication: The compounding math of daily practice makes frequency the single most important variable in AI skill development. Even ten low-quality minutes per day will outperform sporadic intensive sessions over any meaningful time horizon.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: The best insights and phrases often emerge unplanned in the act of doing. Utley's most memorable line from his keynote — 'it's not a pill, it's a skill' — came out spontaneously mid-delivery and surprised even him. He observes this is usually how the best things happen.
original_quote: I didn't plan it. It just came out. Which is usually how the best things happen.
implication: Creative output — including insight and communication — is often a byproduct of showing up and doing the work rather than deliberate pre-planning. Building in space for unscripted iteration in presentations and conversations can surface better ideas than scripted preparation alone.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Once people genuinely internalize the skill of AI, they begin discovering use cases their teachers never anticipated. Utley describes these learner-generated use cases as his favorites — a signal that the skill has truly taken hold and exceeded the scope of formal instruction.
original_quote: Once people start believing they can figure this out, they start finding use cases I've never thought of (those examples are always my favorites!). That's when I know the skill has taken hold.
implication: The goal of AI education isn't to transfer a fixed set of techniques — it's to build the learner's agency and self-belief to the point where they generate novel applications independently. Measure AI program success by learner-generated use cases, not curriculum completion.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Utley's practical prescription for building AI skill prioritizes low-stakes entry points, calendar-blocked daily minimums, and redefining success as reps rather than results. The explicit instruction is to start with something routine and unimportant, not a high-visibility project.
original_quote: Pick one task you do every week and try it with AI today. Not your most critical, career-defining project. Something low-stakes... The lower the stakes, the more you'll actually experiment. That's the bike-riding lesson.
implication: When designing AI onboarding or personal development plans, deliberately route people toward low-consequence tasks first. High-stakes first attempts create performance anxiety that prevents the experimentation necessary to actually learn.
source_ref: It's a Skill, Not a Pill — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy identifies what he calls 'the rising opportunity cost of being human': AI has made it economically more expensive to invest in unmeasurable human activities like parenting, marriage, friendship, and prayer. When AI amplifies output by 10x, 50x, or 100x, an hour spent at dinner no longer costs one unit of output — it costs a hundred. The math has genuinely changed, making presence more costly in purely economic terms than ever before.
original_quote: This is what I now think of as the rising opportunity cost of being human. AI has made it more expensive — in purely economic terms — to invest in the things that can't be measured, optimized, or scaled. Parenting. Marriage. Friendship. Prayer. The things that don't show up on any dashboard but do shape who you become.
implication: Leaders and builders who are most AI-capable face the greatest personal risk. The more you leverage AI, the higher the opportunity cost of every human moment — meaning structural protection of personal life becomes a strategic necessity, not a lifestyle preference.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy reframes what looks like a discipline problem as an economics problem. High achievers feeling pulled away from family and presence aren't failing at willpower — they are rationally responding to asymmetric incentives created by AI amplification. Diagnosing it as a character flaw leads to the wrong prescription: more guilt and harder willpower, both of which will fail because the incentive structure is relentless.
original_quote: When you think 'I lack discipline,' the solution is willpower: try harder, calendar-block more aggressively, feel guilty when you fail. (And you will fail, because the incentives are relentless.) That's not strategy. That's drift. When you recognize 'the opportunity cost of being human has fundamentally changed,' the solution is structural. You don't fight the current. You build a dam.
implication: When you or your team struggle to disconnect from work or AI tools, don't default to moralizing about discipline. Redesign the environment and incentive structure instead — because willpower alone cannot sustainably compete with compounding economic returns.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy draws on Clay Christensen's HBR insight that high achievers systematically over-invest in careers and under-invest in families because of asymmetric feedback loops. Work provides fast, concrete feedback — shipped products, closed deals, promotions. Family and relationships have decades-long feedback loops, and the resilience of loved ones who give the benefit of the doubt actually enables neglect to go undetected longer.
original_quote: When people who have a high need for achievement have an extra half hour of time or an extra ounce of energy, they'll unconsciously allocate it to activities that yield the most tangible accomplishments. And our careers provide the most concrete evidence that we're moving forward.
implication: The absence of a dashboard for being a spouse or parent is not just inconvenient — it is structurally dangerous. Leaders must create artificial feedback mechanisms for the relationships and activities that matter most, because the natural feedback loop is too slow to correct behavior in real time.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy argues that AI has turbocharged the fast-feedback work loop while leaving the slow-feedback human loop completely untouched. Christensen described this allocation failure in 2010 among elite professionals; Jeremy contends that AI has amplified that same failure by orders of magnitude sixteen years later. The structural problem Christensen diagnosed has not been solved — it has been dramatically worsened.
original_quote: What Christensen couldn't have anticipated: AI has turbocharged the fast-feedback loop while leaving the slow-feedback loop completely untouched.
implication: AI does not neutralize the classic high-achiever allocation problem — it accelerates it. Any leader who has adopted AI tools without explicitly redesigning their personal commitment structures is likely drifting toward the same misallocation Christensen's HBS classmates experienced, only faster.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy proposes the 'Odysseus Contract' as the structural solution to rising human opportunity cost. Just as Odysseus had himself bound to the mast before hearing the Sirens — rather than relying on willpower in the moment — the AI age requires binding pre-commitments that hold even when every incentive screams to defect. The contract must be made before the sirens of productivity start singing.
original_quote: That's what I think the AI age requires. Not more discipline. An Odysseus Contract — a binding commitment you make before the sirens of productivity start singing, that holds even when every fiber of your being says 'just one more hour.'
implication: Effective personal governance in the AI age is not about trying harder in the moment — it is about designing constraints before the moment arrives. Leaders should build Odysseus Contracts into their lives: pre-committed, publicly accountable, structurally enforced boundaries around non-delegable human activities.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy identifies three practical components of an Odysseus Contract: (1) make the commitment costly to break through public accountability and social consequences; (2) remove the choice from the moment of temptation by designing the environment in advance; (3) name the exchange rate explicitly by writing down the actual cost of each human investment. The third element is especially important because Christensen showed that allocation failures happen unconsciously.
original_quote: Make the commitment costly to break... Remove the choice from the moment of temptation... Name the exchange rate explicitly. Write this down somewhere you'll see it: 'Every hour I spend with my [kids/spouse/in prayer/at the gym] costs me X units of AI-augmented output. I am choosing to pay that price because [reason].'
implication: Vague intentions to 'be more present' will fail against compounding AI incentives. Effective protection of human priorities requires making the economic tradeoff explicit and visible, removing in-the-moment decision points, and creating social accountability that makes defection embarrassing.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy notes that the people most at risk from rising human opportunity cost are precisely the most capable AI adopters — those who have embraced the 'teammate mindset,' feel the pain of idle agents, and are maximizing AI output. Success at becoming AI-capable is itself a risk factor for humanity-thinning. The very behaviors that produce the greatest professional results create the greatest gravitational pull away from unmeasurable human investment.
original_quote: The scary thing is that the very people who succeed most at becoming AI-capable face the greatest risk. If you've fully embraced the teammate mindset, if you feel the pain of idle agents, if you're keeping the GPUs full — you are precisely the person for whom the opportunity cost of dinnertime is highest.
implication: AI adoption strategy must include personal sustainability planning. The more effectively someone integrates AI into their work, the more deliberately they must design structural protections for their human life — otherwise excellence at AI use and excellence as a human being will increasingly conflict.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy uses Sam Altman as cultural evidence of the structural nature of this problem. Altman — who has publicly called neglecting loved ones 'a very stupid tradeoff' — purchased a ranch in Napa with no cell phone service as a physical barrier against productivity pull, and still reports his daily routine has 'fallen to crap.' Jeremy reads this not as a personal failure but as a structural symptom: even the CEO of OpenAI could not solve this with willpower alone.
original_quote: Even Sam Altman — who has been vocal about calling the neglect of loved ones 'a very stupid tradeoff' — bought a ranch in Napa with no cell phone service just to have a physical barrier between himself and the pull. (Think about that for a second. The CEO of OpenAI had to purchase a $15 million forced-disconnection device.)
implication: If the most informed and intentional person in AI cannot manage the productivity pull through willpower, the problem is systemic rather than personal. This should shift how organizations think about 'always-on' culture and how individuals think about the adequacy of soft commitments versus hard environmental design.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy received over twenty confessional responses from founders, executives, and AI-forward leaders after his previous essay — all describing the same pull away from family and presence. These were not undisciplined people; they were among the most intentional professionals he knows. This response led Jeremy to conclude that if willpower were the answer, these people would have already solved it — and therefore the diagnosis of a discipline problem was wrong.
original_quote: The thing that struck me is, these aren't undisciplined people. These are some of the most intentional, self-aware professionals I know. If willpower were the answer, they'd have solved this already. Which means I missed something last week. I diagnosed the right problem — but I've come to realize that I offered a discipline solution to what is actually an economics problem.
implication: When high-performing, self-aware people share the same struggle at scale, it signals a systemic issue rather than individual failure. Leaders should resist the instinct to address collective behavioral patterns with individual character prescriptions — the pattern itself is evidence that the incentive structure needs redesign.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy distinguishes between his previous 'Big Rocks Before Bots' framework and the Odysseus Contract: the former answers 'what to protect' while the latter answers 'how to make that protection stick.' Pre-allocating reclaimed time to what matters is necessary but insufficient — without a binding mechanism, the allocation will collapse under the real-time incentive pressure of AI-amplified productivity.
original_quote: Last week, I offered 'Big Rocks Before Bots' — pre-allocate your reclaimed time to what matters. That's still true. But it's the what. The Odysseus Contract is the how. It's the binding mechanism that makes the pre-allocation stick when the incentives are screaming at you to defect.
implication: Frameworks for protecting human priorities have two distinct layers: values clarity (what matters) and commitment architecture (how to hold the line). Many leaders have the first layer but lack the second. Without binding mechanisms, values statements are just aspirations that erode under pressure.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy opens with a personal scene of sitting with his wife and experiencing a metacognitive loop — knowing he should be present, thinking about whether he's present, not actually being present because he's thinking about presence. He uses this moment of honest self-observation to establish that the problem is not hypothetical or abstract — it is already operating in the minds of people who care about being human.
original_quote: I was sitting on the couch with my wife the other night. No crisis. No conflict. No drama. Just talking about the day. And in the back of my mind, I could feel it. The temptation to think, This is not productive.
implication: The pull away from presence is not dramatic or obvious — it operates as a subtle background process even in moments of apparent calm. Leaders should not wait for a crisis to notice this dynamic; the metacognitive loop Jeremy describes is a diagnostic signal worth monitoring in everyday life.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy asserts a core human irreplaceability claim as the moral anchor of his argument: no AI model can be a husband to his wife, a father to his daughters, or live his life for him. While the economic cost of protecting those responsibilities has increased, their irreplaceable nature has not changed. This is the foundation for why the rising opportunity cost demands a stronger commitment mechanism rather than a recalibration of values.
original_quote: No model can be a husband to my wife. No model can be a father to my daughters. No model can live my life for me. If the cost of protecting those responsibilities has gone up, then I need a stronger commitment mechanism — not just a stronger will.
implication: AI amplification changes the economics of human investment but not the irreplaceability of human roles. Leaders who accept this distinction will respond by strengthening their commitment architecture; those who don't may unconsciously treat rising opportunity cost as implicit permission to deprioritize irreplaceable human responsibilities.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy nearly cancelled a long-planned family trip to his kids' great-grandparents' house in favor of two business opportunities. The moment of negotiation with himself — rehearsing lines like 'this is a special opportunity' and 'you can't miss this one' — disturbed him more than the opportunities themselves. It revealed a gap between stated values and actual behavior.
original_quote: I almost exchanged a family trip for two business opportunities... And I caught myself negotiating. This is a special opportunity… You can't miss this one… What if you say no?… And for a moment, I almost moved the trip. That moment bothered me more than I expected.
implication: Builders and leaders should audit not just their outputs but their instincts — the moment you start negotiating against your own stated priorities is a signal worth paying attention to.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy applies Stanford Business School's famous application question — 'What matters most to you, and why?' — as a personal accountability test in the AI era. The question is powerful not because there is a right answer, but because it forces alignment between values and behavior. He argues that AI productivity gains make this question more urgent, not less.
original_quote: Stanford Business School has a famous application question: What matters most to you, and why? It's a brutal question (in the best way). Not because there's a right answer. Because it forces alignment. Now layer AI on top of that. If AI gives us massive productivity gains, then we have to ask: What are those gains for?
implication: Leaders adopting AI should answer the Stanford question first — before optimizing workflows — so that productivity gains serve stated values rather than default to more output.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy identifies a critical inversion in AI adoption: it is possible to maximize AI output — 'keep the GPUs full' — while simultaneously hollowing out one's humanity. Both conditions can coexist, and most people have not yet reckoned with this tension. Efficiency and human fullness are not the same thing.
original_quote: I can keep the GPUs full while emptying parts of my humanity. That's the uncomfortable part. Both can be true at the same time. Most of us haven't reckoned with that yet.
implication: Optimizing AI utilization without intentionally protecting human roles and relationships can produce a worker who is more productive and less whole — a trade most people would reject if they named it explicitly.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: When Jeremy asked members of an advanced AI group what they were doing with time reclaimed by AI, the overwhelming answer was 'more work.' Many of these same people list family, faith, health, and friendship as their top values. The disconnect between stated values and actual time allocation is the core problem he is naming.
original_quote: I asked a simple question: what are you doing with your reclaimed time?... The overwhelming answer: more work. No judgment. If your goal is more output, AI is extraordinary. But many of us say our top values are family, faith, health, friendship, service.
implication: AI amplifies existing time-allocation patterns rather than correcting them. If you were already overinvesting in work before AI, AI will deepen that imbalance unless you actively redirect the gains.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy reframes the time scarcity problem in the AI era as a values-allocation problem, not a tooling problem. Even when AI compresses eight weeks of work into 90 minutes, people still report not having time for what matters most. The constraint is not time — it is intentional direction of reclaimed time.
original_quote: We can now do in 90 minutes what teams used to quote as 8 weeks, and still end up telling ourselves we 'don't have time' for what matters most. That's not a tooling problem. That's a values-allocation problem.
implication: Giving people better tools without helping them decide where to direct the freed capacity will not produce better lives — only faster work. Leaders and individuals must treat time reallocation as a deliberate act.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy extends Parkinson's Law — 'work expands to fill the time available' — to the AI era, arguing that freed time will be consumed by more work unless you pre-decide its destination. He applies this principle both at the enterprise level (innovation, new markets) and personally (family, faith, health). Without pre-commitment, reclaimed hours drift toward whatever pings, not what matters.
original_quote: You free up time... and work expands to consume the gain... If I don't pre-decide where my regained hours go, they won't drift toward what I value. They'll drift toward what pings.
implication: Pre-allocation of reclaimed AI time is not optional — it is the mechanism that converts efficiency gains into life gains. Both organizations and individuals need to make this decision before the time is freed, not after.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy draws a sharp distinction between what AI can do 'for' him versus what it can do 'as' him. Being a father, husband, best friend, and follower of Jesus Christ are roles that are irreducibly his — no agent or automation can perform them in his place. This framing gives concrete shape to the human-AI boundary.
original_quote: No one else can be a father to my kids. No one else can be a husband to my wife. No one else can be a best friend to my best friends. No one else can follow Jesus Christ on my behalf. AI can do a shocking number of things for me. It cannot do those things as me. Those are irreducibly mine.
implication: Identifying your 'irreducibles' — roles no one can perform for you — is a practical first step to protecting what matters most in an AI-augmented life. These roles deserve calendar protection, not leftover time.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy adapts the classic 'big rocks first' metaphor for the AI era: place human priorities in the calendar before allocating AI-reclaimed capacity to additional work. The metaphor holds — if sand (tasks, pings, work expansion) fills the jar first, the rocks (family, faith, health) won't fit. AI makes the sand faster and more plentiful, raising the stakes.
original_quote: The AI version is simple: place your human priorities first, then allocate your newfound capacity gains accordingly.
implication: The big-rocks metaphor is more actionable than ever in an AI context. Scheduling non-delegable roles before planning AI-augmented work sprints is a structural habit that protects values at scale.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy offers a four-step personal framework for redirecting AI productivity gains toward stated values: (1) name your non-delegable roles, (2) pre-allocate your next eight reclaimed hours, (3) block those commitments on your actual calendar, and (4) run a weekly integrity check by asking whether AI helped you become more of what you value.
original_quote: Name your non-delegables... Pre-allocate your next 8 reclaimed hours... Put those blocks on your actual calendar... Run a weekly integrity check. Ask: 'Did AI help me become more of what I say I value?'
implication: This four-step framework gives creators and leaders a repeatable system for converting AI efficiency into life alignment, not just output expansion. The weekly review question is the accountability mechanism that keeps the system honest.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy provides a concrete AI prompt designed to turn reclaimed time into values-aligned calendar blocks. The prompt asks the AI to take the user's stated values and non-delegable roles and design a seven-day schedule that prioritizes those commitments first, complete with guardrails and a weekly review question.
original_quote: "I'm using AI to reclaim time. My top values are [list]. My non-delegable roles are [list]. Help me design my next 7 days so reclaimed time goes to these priorities first. Give me specific calendar blocks, guardrails, and one weekly review question."
implication: AI can be turned on itself — used not just to accelerate work but to actively design a life aligned with values. This prompt pattern demonstrates that the tool is only as human-centered as the intention behind the prompt.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy reflects on a recurring pattern across his life stages — school, early career, kids, advancement, and now AI — where there has always been a 'perfectly reasonable excuse' for not yet becoming the person he hopes to be. He is determined not to let AI become the latest version of that excuse.
original_quote: It used to be school. Then early career. Then kids. Then advancement. Now AI. At every stage, there's been a perfectly reasonable excuse for why I'm not yet the person I hope to be. I don't want AI to become the next excuse.
implication: AI is neither a villain nor a savior in the story of who you become — it is the latest context in which you choose alignment or drift. Naming this pattern prevents the rationalization before it takes hold.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy poses a single governing question for the AI era: 'Whatever you value most... are you now more of it?' This question functions as a recurring integrity test that cuts through productivity metrics, tool adoption rates, and output volume to ask what actually changed in a person's life.
original_quote: Whatever you value most... are you now more of it?
implication: This question is a simple but powerful filter for evaluating any technology adoption. Leaders can apply it at team and organizational levels: does AI make us more of what we say we are as a company, or just faster at what we were already doing?
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy is explicit about his faith as a non-negotiable personal value and uses it as a test case for AI alignment. He asks not whether AI is making him a more efficient worker, but whether it is helping him become a more wholehearted disciple — real, daily, and relational rather than performative or theoretical.
original_quote: I'm a Christian. I want to be a wholehearted disciple of Jesus Christ. Not performative. Not theoretical. Real. In daily life. In relationships. In how I spend my attention. And I have to ask myself: is AI helping me become more of that man, or just a more efficient worker?
implication: The test of AI's value is not productivity — it is formation. Whatever your deepest commitments are, the right question is whether AI is helping you grow into them or simply accelerating a version of yourself that is already misaligned.
source_ref: Are You More of What You Value? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy defines 'wherewithal moments' as flashes of realization, mid-struggle, that you've been doing something alone that you didn't need to do alone. These moments are hard to manufacture but they compound over time, making each subsequent moment slightly easier to reach. The trigger is almost always discomfort — being stuck, overwhelmed, or failing — not comfort or cruise control.
original_quote: They're those flashes where you suddenly realize, mid-struggle, that you've been doing something alone that you didn't need to do alone. They're hard to manufacture. But they compound.
implication: Leaders and creators should reframe the feeling of being stuck not as a signal to work harder, but as a cue to ask: 'Am I doing this alone because I have to, or because I forgot I don't have to?'
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: The biggest barrier to becoming AI-native isn't skill or access — it's the deep conditioning of doing cognitive work alone. Most people have been trained through years of 'unassisted living' to default to solo effort, and breaking that conditioning requires deliberate pattern interruption rather than just learning new tools.
original_quote: The biggest barrier to becoming AI native isn't skill or access, but the deep conditioning of doing cognitive work alone.
implication: Organizations investing in AI tools should focus as much on behavioral and cultural conditioning as on tooling and training — the default to solo work is the real adoption blocker.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy used Claude Code with sub-agents to generate 90 pages of research per chapter for his new book — academic studies, real-world examples, counterarguments, and data — in a fraction of the time his first book required. He had been stuck for weeks not because the technology was unavailable, but because he hadn't thought to ask.
original_quote: I ended up with 90 pages of research per chapter. Academic studies. Real-world examples. Counterarguments. Data I never would have found on my own — and certainly not in the time I had. I'd been stuck for weeks. Not because the technology wasn't available. Because I hadn't thought to ask.
implication: AI agents can dramatically compress research timelines for knowledge workers — but the bottleneck is prompt imagination, not technical capability. The act of asking is the unlock.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: While traveling to deliver a keynote, Jeremy instructed Claude Code to imagine 100 book cover creative directions, write three image generation prompts for each, call Gemini's image API, and save all results to a desktop folder — generating 300 cover concepts for $11.95 while he was on stage. The goal wasn't to get a finished product, but to generate raw material to react to and begin discovering what he actually wanted.
original_quote: 300 book cover concepts. Organized by creative direction. Total cost: $11.95 in API tokens. Were most of them bad? Yes. Were some of them surprisingly interesting? Also yes. Did I have raw material to react to — to start discovering what I actually wanted? Absolutely.
implication: AI-generated volume is most valuable not as a final output but as a divergent starting point — raw material that helps creators identify their preferences through reaction rather than blank-page invention.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy reframes the role of the modern knowledge worker from 'individual contributor' to 'manager with a team.' He had been ghosting his PR agent for months due to bandwidth constraints, spending 45 minutes minimum per response — until he delegated the task to Claude in five minutes, getting three on-brand options per question. The realization: 'That's handmade artisanal Jeremy who doesn't have the bandwidth. With AI, Augmented-I DO have bandwidth.'
original_quote: That's 'handmade artisanal Jeremy' who doesn't have the bandwidth. With AI, Augmented-I DO have bandwidth. I'd been operating as an individual contributor when I had a team available.
implication: Professionals who treat themselves as solo operators are leaving team capacity on the table. Reframing from 'individual contributor' to 'manager with AI agents' is a practical identity shift that unlocks new leverage.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Wherewithal moments don't emerge from planning or calm — they emerge from struggle. Every example Jeremy shares (drowning in research, no time for design, months of dropped PR balls) followed a period of genuine failure or overwhelm. Discomfort is the diagnostic signal that you're working alone on something you don't have to do alone.
original_quote: Nobody has a wherewithal moment while cruising. You have them when you're struggling. Which means — and this is the uncomfortable part — the feeling of being stuck is actually a signal. It's your cue to ask: 'Am I doing this alone because I have to, or because I forgot I don't have to?'
implication: Rather than treating overwhelm as a productivity failure, leaders should treat it as a prompt-design opportunity — a moment to define what to hand off and to whom.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: The wherewithal reflex — remembering you have a team and deploying it — develops through repetition and compounds non-linearly. Jeremy compares it to learning to notice a bird species for the first time: once you see it, you start seeing it everywhere. He acknowledges he still forgets constantly, but forgets slightly less than six months prior.
original_quote: Not linearly — it's more like learning to notice a bird you'd never seen before. Once you see it once, you start seeing it everywhere. I'm not there yet. I still forget constantly. But I forget slightly less than I did six months ago.
implication: Building AI-native habits is a slow, compounding process — not a switch to flip. The goal isn't perfection but incrementally shrinking the gap between struggle and delegation reflex.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy offers a minimal viable prompt for activating AI as a working teammate: 'I have 45 minutes in a meeting. While I'm gone, I'd like you to [task]. When I come back, have three options ready for me to react to.' The design principle is reaction over creation — returning to options rather than starting from scratch.
original_quote: 'I have 45 minutes in a meeting. While I'm gone, I'd like you to [describe the task]. When I come back, have three options ready for me to react to.'
implication: The 'three options to react to' structure is a repeatable prompt pattern that lowers blank-page friction and leverages the human advantage of evaluation over generation.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy's first book, Ideaflow, was built on twelve years of reading — dog-eared pages, highlighted passages, notes half-forgotten. Most of those stories went directly from reading into the manuscript, without lodging in his long-term memory. He even paused listening to the audiobook of his own book to take notes, because he couldn't hold the whole thing in his head.
original_quote: True story: I actually paused reading the audiobook multiple times to take notes… on my own book. The director thought that was strange. But I can't imagine any author is walking around with an entire book in their head!
implication: Even expert authors don't carry their own work in active memory — externalizing knowledge into systems (notes, agents, retrieval tools) is not a crutch but a structural necessity for knowledge work at scale.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Activating AI team capacity doesn't require an elaborate workflow or a free hour — it requires five minutes of clarity. Jeremy nearly arrived empty-handed to a weekly learning cohort meeting, then opened two Claude Code windows five minutes before the call. By the time the meeting ended, he had five proposed article directions with supporting evidence pulled from his own conversations.
original_quote: You don't need an hour. You need five minutes of clarity. Not time to build some elaborate workflow. Just five minutes where you remember: I'm not an individual contributor. I have a team.
implication: The activation energy for AI leverage is lower than most people assume — the constraint is not time or skill but the five-minute decision to delegate before defaulting to solo effort.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy frames the question 'What could I have working while I'm away?' as more actionable than 'What could AI do for me?' The first is concrete and time-bound; the second is abstract and paralyzing. The question reorients the user from passive tool consumer to active delegation manager.
original_quote: Before your next meeting, ask yourself: 'What could I have working while I'm away?' Not 'What could AI do for me?' — that's too abstract. 'What could I have working while I'm away?'
implication: Reframing AI prompts as delegation decisions — tied to real time windows and specific outputs — makes the practice more concrete, habitual, and immediately actionable for busy professionals.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy used Claude Code's ability to read his own blog posts and call transcripts to generate PR responses in his voice, complete with quotable hooks — in about five minutes of active time during a live workshop. This illustrates how personal knowledge artifacts (writing, transcripts) can serve as voice-training material for AI to act as a credible proxy.
original_quote: I said: 'Read all my blog posts and call transcripts. Then read these questions from my PR agent. Craft three possible responses to each question, in my voice, with the kind of hooks that work for PR.'
implication: Creators who consistently produce written or spoken content are building a latent AI training asset — their body of work can be leveraged to generate on-brand outputs with minimal active effort.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Jeremy introduced a personal concept called 'Augmented-I' as a reframe for what AI-assisted work actually represents — not a dilution of the self, but an augmented version with expanded bandwidth. The contrast is 'handmade artisanal Jeremy,' who is bandwidth-constrained, versus 'Augmented-I,' who has team capacity.
original_quote: That's 'handmade artisanal Jeremy' who doesn't have the bandwidth. With AI, Augmented-I DO have bandwidth.
implication: The 'Augmented-I' framing helps professionals reconcile authenticity concerns about AI assistance — reframing it as expanded capacity rather than outsourced identity, which may lower psychological resistance to delegation.
source_ref: I Have a Team (A Collection of Wherewithal Moments) — Methods of the Masters blog

content: The biggest barrier to becoming AI-native is not technical skill, access, or prompting ability — it is that people have spent their entire lives doing cognitive work alone and are simply conditioned to unassisted living. This conditioning runs so deep that even when unlimited intelligence becomes available for the cost of a fancy coffee, most people don't use it — not because they can't, but because they don't think of it.
original_quote: We have become accustomed to unassisted living. We are not used to having abundant intelligence available to us. We've spent our entire careers — our entire lives — doing cognitive work alone. Handmade. Artisanal. Unassisted.
implication: Builders and leaders should not assume that access or training is the bottleneck to AI adoption. The real work is breaking a deeply ingrained habit of going it alone — which requires deliberate re-conditioning, not just tool availability.
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Ethan Mollick's framing that 'talent is abundant and cheap, so what's scarce is knowing what to ask for' captures step two of AI adoption — but misses step one. The actual bottleneck, according to Utley, is the prior realization that you can ask at all. Most people never even reach the question-design stage because they haven't internalized that a capable assistant exists.
original_quote: He's right. But I think he's describing step two. Step one — the actual bottleneck — is knowing you can ask at all.
implication: Leaders designing AI onboarding or culture change should focus first on shifting identity and awareness — helping people realize they have a team — before investing in prompt engineering or question-quality training.
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Capabilities once reserved for heads of state — like having a knowledgeable, context-aware assistant available at all times — are now accessible to anyone with a wifi connection for roughly $20 a month. Utley uses the Churchill bathtub dictation scene as a vivid illustration of what AI now makes universally available. The democratization of intelligence is as significant as the electrification of fans in rural India.
original_quote: What was once reserved for leaders of nation states is now available to anyone with a wifi connection... What was only available to the very few is now available to the masses. The same is true of intelligence.
implication: The competitive moat is no longer access to intelligence — it is the willingness and habit of deploying it. Anyone who fails to act on this democratization is voluntarily operating at a disadvantage that was once structural and unavoidable.
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Brice Challamel, former head of AI at Moderna who trained 7,000 employees, reframed his role in his own household by saying 'I'm no longer an individual contributor to my household. I'm a team of five.' He used AI as a note-taker, real-time coach, developmental psychologist, and creative partner across 16 parent-teacher conferences — improving as a parent over the course of a single day through continuous AI-assisted feedback.
original_quote: I'm no longer an individual contributor to my household. I'm a team of five.
implication: The AI-native mindset applies to personal and family life, not just professional work. Leaders should model and practice multi-agent thinking in all domains — this is how the identity shift from individual contributor to team leader becomes embodied rather than theoretical.
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Bryan McCann, who built Salesforce's AI program before founding You.com, operated under the mantra 'keep the GPUs full' — meaning he and his team constantly thought about what meaningful tasks to assign AI while they were away, sleeping, or in meetings. Fridays were particularly stressful because he had to identify tasks hard enough to occupy nearly unlimited intelligence for 48 hours over the weekend.
original_quote: Keep the GPUs full... Bryan said that Fridays are particularly stressful. On Thursday, he only has to think of a hard enough task for AI to handle for 12 hours until he's back. But on Friday? If he wants a weekend, he has to think of a task hard enough to keep almost unlimited intelligence meaningfully occupied for 48 hours.
implication: Highly AI-native practitioners experience a new kind of productive stress: the pressure to find tasks worthy of nearly unlimited intelligence. This is a useful diagnostic — if your weekends feel relaxing rather than like a missed deployment opportunity, you likely haven't yet internalized what's available.
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Utley uses 'the pain of idle agents' as a diagnostic test for whether someone has truly grasped what AI makes available. If you regularly go to bed, go into meetings, or go on vacation without deploying AI to work on your behalf, you haven't yet understood the magnitude of the opportunity — regardless of how much you know about AI.
original_quote: If you don't feel the pain of idle agents — if you regularly go to bed, go to meetings, go on vacation without deploying intelligence to work on your behalf — you haven't grasped what's available to you. Not yet.
implication: The pain of idle agents is a practical self-assessment tool. Leaders and creators can ask themselves: 'When I step away, is intelligence working for me?' If the answer is consistently no, the problem is not capability — it is habit and identity.
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: The real bottleneck to AI leverage is not intelligence, not prompting skill, and not even knowing what to ask — it is having the wherewithal to remember, in the moment before a meeting or task, that you have a team available to you. Utley reports that this transition requires only about five minutes of intentional setup, but yields 90 minutes of work product he would otherwise have produced alone.
original_quote: The primary bottleneck is having the wherewithal to remember you have a team. Most people don't. Not because the team isn't available. Because they've grown so accustomed to unassisted living that they don't even notice the arbitrary ceiling.
implication: The five-minute habit of asking 'what can I deploy before I go into this meeting?' is one of the highest-leverage micro-practices available to any knowledge worker. The constraint is not time, skill, or access — it is a brief moment of intentional remembering.
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Utley describes doing his best thinking in the bathtub but being unable to capture that thinking because he can't bring electronics — a frustration that led him to the realization that AI now functions as the context-aware, always-available assistant that was previously only accessible to figures like Churchill. This insight reframes AI not as a productivity tool but as a personal cognitive infrastructure.
original_quote: I have an assistant who has all of my contacts and knows my audience and knows what I said last time and knows my objectives. It's called AI. And what was only available to someone like Winston Churchill a generation ago is now available to all of us for the price of — max — $20 a month.
implication: Framing AI as personal cognitive infrastructure — rather than a software feature — changes how creators and leaders think about investment, reliance, and integration. It shifts the question from 'should I use this tool?' to 'how do I build a working relationship with this assistant?'
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: The contrast between Bryan McCann's stressful Fridays and most people's relaxing weekends is Utley's sharpest illustration of the gap between those who have internalized AI's potential and those who haven't. Both groups have equal access to the same technology — the difference is entirely cognitive and habitual, not structural.
original_quote: Bryan's Fridays are stressful. Most people's weekends are relaxing. That's the difference between someone who has grasped what's available and someone who hasn't.
implication: Organizations trying to build AI culture should study the attitudinal and habitual differences between high-deployers and non-deployers — the gap is not in tools or training, but in whether people have internalized the identity of someone who has a team working for them around the clock.
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Utley practices what he preaches by running Claude Code agents in the background during his weekly AI Junto meetings — one reading call transcripts to propose article directions, another doing similar work on a separate project. This is not described as an advanced or expert move, but as something requiring only five minutes of intentional setup before a meeting.
original_quote: While we were talking, I had Claude Code working on a couple tasks in the background... One agent spent about 10 minutes reading through my call transcripts and proposing article directions. Another was doing similar work on a different project. All of that was happening while I was in the meeting. Because I took five minutes beforehand to remember, 'I'm not an individual contributor anymore.'
implication: Multi-agent background deployment during meetings is a concrete, accessible practice — not a futuristic concept. The barrier is the five-minute intention-setting habit, not technical expertise. Anyone can start this today.
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Utley frames the AI adoption challenge as one of identity before skill: moving from seeing yourself as an 'individual contributor' to seeing yourself as a 'team leader' is the foundational cognitive shift. Until that identity shift occurs, all the prompting skill and access in the world will go underutilized because the person simply won't think to deploy it.
original_quote: I'm not an individual contributor anymore.
implication: AI training and onboarding programs that focus primarily on skills and tools without addressing identity are likely to underperform. The most leveraged intervention may be helping people viscerally internalize the phrase 'I am not an individual contributor anymore' before teaching them anything else.
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Brice Challamel's parent-teacher conference approach illustrates that AI augmentation compounds within a single session — he became a better parent over the course of 16 meetings because of continuous real-time coaching between each one. This is qualitatively different from using AI to produce a final output; it represents AI as a live feedback loop that accelerates in-session learning.
original_quote: He said he became a better parent over the course of those 16 meetings because of the continuous coaching.
implication: The most underexplored use of AI may not be output generation but real-time performance coaching. Leaders, salespeople, teachers, and parents can use AI as a between-rep coach — compressing the feedback loop that normally takes weeks or years into a single afternoon.
source_ref: Do You Feel The Pain Of Idle Agents? — Methods of the Masters blog

content: AI's fundamental limitation is that it never initiates — it only responds when asked. Sam Altman confirmed this directly: AI systems 'don't do anything unless you ask' and have no 'sense of agency or autonomy.' In an age of increasingly capable AI, the humans who win will be the ones who ask first and act without being prompted.
original_quote: They don't do anything unless you ask. They're just sitting there waiting. They don't have a sense of agency or autonomy…
implication: Human initiative — the act of noticing and acting before being asked — is structurally impossible for AI to replicate. Professionals who cultivate proactive behavior are building a durable competitive advantage, not just a soft skill.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Jeremy's turnaround at BCG came not from increased intelligence or longer hours, but from adopting a single behavioral rule: always volunteer for unclear follow-ups. His father's advice — 'always leave with the monkey on your back' — reframed initiative as a systematic practice rather than a personality trait. Within three months, he moved from worst performer to top quartile.
original_quote: Every meeting ends with tasks, next steps, follow-ups. When it's unclear who's doing what, you volunteer. Always. Never let anybody else leave with the monkey on their back.
implication: Career acceleration is often less about raw talent and more about consistent, visible initiative. Deliberately volunteering for unglamorous work builds trust and reputation faster than performing well on assigned tasks alone.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Even 'agentic AI' operates toward goals that must first be given by a human. Ethan Mollick's definition — AI that plans and acts autonomously 'toward given goals' — reveals that goal-setting and caring about outcomes remain irreducibly human responsibilities. Someone still has to 'be the prompt.'
original_quote: AI that can plan and act autonomously toward given goals. Toward given goals. Someone still has to give them. Someone has to care first. Someone has to be the prompt.
implication: Even as AI autonomy increases, the human role shifts upstream to goal-setting, problem-framing, and caring about outcomes — making problem-finding skills more valuable, not less.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Josh To, who leads Meta's Orion AR glasses project, started in customer service at Google. His differentiator was a personal rule: complete his hour-long job in 45 minutes, then spend the final 15 minutes finding and solving problems nobody asked him to solve. This self-imposed initiative practice was the engine of his career trajectory.
original_quote: He gave himself a rule. Do his hour-long job in 45 minutes, then spend the last 15 minutes finding problems nobody asked him to solve. Taking initiative.
implication: Structuring discretionary time — even 15 minutes daily — around unsolicited problem-solving is a repeatable career strategy accessible at any level. Initiative doesn't require permission or seniority.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Jeremy frames initiative as a five-step 'Initiative Stack' he calls the 5 N's: Notice, Name, Nudge, Nail, and Narrate. The sequence moves from personal friction-spotting through to broadcasting impact so others can replicate the solution. Each step is designed to be actionable before any formal approval or assignment.
original_quote: Notice — Keep a bug list. What frustrates you? What wastes time? AI will never notice; only you can. Name — Identify the unasked question. Nudge — Start before the meeting. Draft the doc. Build the prototype at lunch. Nail — Deliver the 8-hour proof. Narrate — Broadcast impact. Show before/after. Let others copy.
implication: Teams and individuals can use the 5 N's as an operational framework for converting personal frustration into organizational value — without waiting for top-down mandates or resource allocation.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Maintaining a 'bug list' — a running log of daily frictions and frustrations — is the foundational act of proactive innovation. Because AI cannot notice what annoys or wastes time in a specific human context, personal observation of friction is a uniquely human input. The bug list converts ambient irritation into actionable opportunity.
original_quote: Notice — Keep a bug list. What frustrates you? What wastes time? AI will never notice; only you can.
implication: Professionals at all levels should treat their daily frustrations as a strategic asset, not background noise. A bug list is a low-cost, high-signal source of innovation opportunities that requires no special access or authority.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Jeremy argues that for junior professionals, initiative is the only 'unfakeable' advantage in an AI-saturated workplace. Peers can use ChatGPT to polish deliverables, but no AI can notice an untracked pattern, volunteer for an unglamorous project, or show up without being asked. Proactivity is structurally resistant to AI substitution.
original_quote: This is your only unfakeable advantage. Sure, peers can use ChatGPT to polish reports. But ChatGPT can't notice the pattern nobody's tracking. ChatGPT can't volunteer for the unglamorous project that actually matters.
implication: Junior employees should stop competing on output polish — where AI has an edge — and instead double down on initiative, visibility, and problem-finding, where AI cannot compete.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: For senior professionals, staying indispensable in the AI era requires being three problems ahead with AI-powered proofs already in hand — not waiting for more autonomous agents. The strategy is to use AI to accelerate execution on self-identified problems before others have even named them.
original_quote: While others wait for 'more autonomous agents,' you're already three problems ahead, with AI-powered proofs in hand.
implication: Senior leaders should reframe their role from managing assigned problems to proactively identifying and prototype-testing solutions to emerging issues — using AI as an execution accelerator for self-directed work.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Jeremy advocates for an '8-hour proof' approach to new ideas: instead of asking for budget or formal approval, build a working stub in a day. This reframes prototyping as a personal act of initiative rather than an organizational process, and collapses the timeline from weeks to hours.
original_quote: Remember my '8 weeks → 8 hours' revelation? Stop asking for budget; build a working stub in a day.
implication: Waiting for organizational approval before testing an idea is a form of learned helplessness. A same-day prototype signals seriousness, generates real feedback, and bypasses the permission economy entirely.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Narrating impact — sharing solutions broadly and letting others copy them — multiplies the value of individual initiative. Jeremy cites Adam at the National Park Service, whose 45-minute AI tool now saves 20 years of labor annually because he shared it with others in need. Visibility and generosity of sharing are force multipliers for initiative.
original_quote: Narrate — Broadcast impact. Show before/after. Let others copy. Remember Adam at the National Park Service? His 45-minute tool now saves 20 years of labor annually because he shared it with others in need!
implication: Building a solution in private captures only a fraction of its potential value. Deliberately broadcasting what you built — and making it easy for others to adopt — transforms individual initiative into organizational-scale impact.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: The most powerful prompt is not the one typed into an AI tool — it is the internal prompt a person gives themselves: 'What needs doing that nobody's asking for?' This reframes the concept of prompting from a technical AI skill to a fundamental posture of proactive leadership.
original_quote: The most powerful prompt isn't the one you type into ChatGPT. It's the one you give yourself: What needs doing that nobody's asking for?
implication: Developing the habit of self-prompting — regularly asking what unseen problems deserve attention — is a metacognitive practice that compounds over a career and cannot be outsourced to any tool.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Jeremy proposes a 15-minute daily 'Initiative Drill' as a concrete habit: five minutes identifying daily frictions, two minutes selecting the smallest quick win, six minutes prompting AI for an 8-hour solution, and two minutes committing publicly to a first pass. The drill ends with a specific message template that signals ongoing momentum to a manager.
original_quote: Bug sweep (5 min): Write three daily frictions at work. Pick one (2 min): Circle the smallest, quickest win. AI assist (6 min)… Stake the monkey (2 min): Tell your boss: 'I'll bring a first pass by tomorrow.'
implication: Initiative can be systematized into a repeatable daily habit rather than left to motivation or circumstance. A 15-minute structured drill converts passive frustration into committed action and visible accountability.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Adding a P.S. to a follow-up message — signaling the next problem you're already tracking — trains colleagues and managers to expect continuous initiative rather than one-off contributions. This small communication habit shapes others' perception of you as someone with sustained momentum.
original_quote: P.S. Also keeping an eye on [next bug from your list]. That P.S.? It signals momentum. You're training them to expect initiative.
implication: Professional reputation is built incrementally through small signals as much as large deliverables. Deliberately communicating what you're watching next turns a single act of initiative into a pattern others come to rely on.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: When a team member asks a leader for an answer, the most powerful response is often 'Have you tried AI?' rather than providing the answer directly. This builds capability instead of dependency, turning a single interaction into a repeatable habit. The goal of leadership is to develop others' ability to find answers, not to be the person everyone must ask for everything.
original_quote: If you would actually rather have them be able—which I think is what leadership is really about, unleashing the capabilities of others—then you want to get yourself out of the loop as much as possible.
implication: Leaders should treat 'Have you tried AI?' as a default reflex, not a deflection. Answering every question yourself creates a bottleneck; redirecting to AI builds a scalable, capable team.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Even leaders who know the answer may be better served by asking 'Have you tried AI?' rather than providing it. Answering the question solves the immediate problem; redirecting to AI builds a long-term muscle and habit. The distinction is between being a solution provider versus being a capability builder.
original_quote: Even if you do know the answer, an exceptional leader may actually prefer to ask 'Have you tried AI?' Because you're not just solving their immediate problem. You're building a muscle. You're creating a habit.
implication: Knowing the answer and sharing it are not always the same good leadership move. Withholding the direct answer in favor of an AI redirect is sometimes the higher-leverage choice.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Asking 'Have you tried AI?' gives team members three distinct gifts: it opens their mind to a resource they forgot they had, grants them explicit permission to use AI, and creates a learning moment about how to collaborate with AI on that type of problem. Each of these benefits compounds over time in ways that a direct answer never could.
original_quote: When you ask 'Have you tried AI?' you're giving your team three gifts: It Opens Their Mind... It Gives Them Permission... It Creates a Learning Moment.
implication: A single redirecting question can deliver more long-term value than a direct answer. Leaders should think of 'Have you tried AI?' as a teaching intervention, not a cop-out.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Many employees don't use AI not because they lack access, but because they haven't received explicit permission from their leaders. When a leader actively asks 'Have you tried AI?', it signals that using AI is not just acceptable but expected. This cultural signal is often the missing ingredient for broader AI adoption within teams.
original_quote: Even if they have thought of it, there's often a question: 'Is it irresponsible for me to use AI for this?' 'Does my boss want me to?' When you ask the question—when you explicitly suggest they try AI—you're giving them permission. You're signaling: Yes, this is not only okay, it's expected.
implication: Organizational AI adoption often stalls not from lack of tools but lack of cultural permission. Leaders can unlock adoption simply by explicitly endorsing AI use in everyday conversations.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: AI should be framed not as a search engine or answer machine but as a teammate — an expert, coach, and creative partner available 24/7. When employees engage AI with a specific problem, they don't just get an answer; they learn how to collaborate with AI on that class of problem, building transferable skills that compound over time.
original_quote: Here's what most people miss: AI isn't a tool that gives answers; it's a teammate: an expert, a coach, a creative partner. When a member of your team invites AI into a specific problem, they don't just get an answer—they discover how to work with AI as a teammate on this type of problem.
implication: Reframing AI from 'tool' to 'teammate' changes how people engage with it — more iteratively, more collaboratively, and more skillfully. Leaders and trainers should adopt this framing explicitly.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Leaders who redirect team members to AI create a flywheel of success stories they can then amplify publicly. By following up with 'How did AI help?' and celebrating those stories, leaders normalize AI use and multiply its impact across the organization. This approach turns individual experiments into collective learning.
original_quote: Here's the move: When your teammate comes back with their work product, ask a follow-up question: 'How did AI help?' Then—and this is important—get them to share with others how AI helped them. Even better, brag on them publicly, yourself. This is how you create a flywheel.
implication: AI success stories are underutilized leadership tools. Systematically collecting and sharing them accelerates adoption, builds psychological safety, and creates a culture where AI use is the norm.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Cheryl Eckhardt at the National Park Service exemplifies a model of AI leadership that amplifies others rather than answering questions herself. Her monthly office hours spotlight community members sharing what they've built with AI, creating a distributed learning model. Jeremy frames this as 'brilliant leadership' because it scales impact beyond what any single expert could achieve.
original_quote: She has monthly office hours where she spotlights community members sharing what they've built with AI. She doesn't answer questions—she amplifies the people who've already figured stuff out. That's brilliant leadership.
implication: The most effective AI leaders in organizations may not be the most technically skilled — they're the ones who create platforms for others to share and spread AI capability.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Jeremy recounts a personal story of paying thousands of dollars for an AI course, repeatedly waiting for TA responses — only to be told each time: 'Have you asked ChatGPT?' This frustrating loop eventually produced a key insight: the expert he needed was already accessible without waiting. The lesson is that dependency on human gatekeepers for AI-answerable questions is unnecessary and self-imposed.
original_quote: It took me—embarrassingly—several rounds of this before it finally clicked: They're just going to keep asking if I've tried ChatGPT. I don't have to wait for the TA. The expert I need is already in my pocket.
implication: Many professionals are still waiting for permission or a human intermediary before consulting AI. Recognizing that AI is an always-available expert removes a major friction point in learning and problem-solving.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Leaders often resist asking 'Have you tried AI?' because it feels like admitting they don't know the answer or abandoning their responsibility. Jeremy reframes this fear: always being the answer source is not scalable leadership, it's a bottleneck. True leadership is about making others capable, not indispensable to every decision.
original_quote: Doesn't it look like I don't know the answer? And here's the deeper fear: Isn't this a relinquishment of my 'leadership responsibility'? But the truth is, that's only true if you want to always be the person everybody has to ask for everything.
implication: The instinct to always provide answers feels like leadership but can actually limit team growth. Leaders should audit whether their helpfulness is building capability or creating dependency.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Even Jeremy himself — a vocal AI advocate — keeps a Post-it note on his screen that says 'Have you tried ChatGPT?' as a personal reminder. The fact that he forgets to use AI despite deeply believing in it illustrates that building the reflex requires deliberate environmental cues, not just intellectual conviction.
original_quote: I mean, I literally have a post-it note on my screen that's been there for two years that says 'Have you tried ChatGPT?' Because even I forget.
implication: Behavioral change around AI adoption requires environmental design, not just mindset shifts. Physical or digital reminders that prompt AI-first thinking are practical tools, not gimmicks.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Jeremy frames the discomfort leaders feel when first asking 'Have you tried AI?' as a productive signal: it's the sensation of an old leadership paradigm breaking down. This reframe transforms the awkwardness from a reason to avoid the behavior into evidence that meaningful change is occurring.
original_quote: The first few times will feel weird. That uncomfortable feeling? That's your old leadership paradigm dying. Good riddance.
implication: Discomfort when changing leadership behavior around AI is a feature, not a bug. Leaders should interpret friction as a sign of genuine behavioral change rather than evidence they're doing something wrong.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: The ultimate organizational goal of consistently asking 'Have you tried AI?' is a culture where the default instinct is 'ask AI first' rather than 'ask the boss.' This shift reduces managerial load, speeds problem-solving, and builds a more resilient, self-sufficient organization. It requires consistent practice over weeks, not a single conversation.
original_quote: You're giving your organization the most important gift of all: a culture where the default instinct isn't 'ask the boss' but 'ask AI first.'
implication: Culture change around AI is a long game that requires leaders to model and reinforce the behavior repeatedly. A 2-3 week sustained practice is suggested as the minimum to establish the new pattern.
source_ref: Have You Tried AI? — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: People apply a profound double standard to AI versus human collaborators. When a human teammate misses something or makes an error, we give them the benefit of the doubt, clarify, and iterate. When AI does the exact same thing, we conclude it is incompetent or broken.
original_quote: That's the double standard that's killing your AI collaboration.
implication: Leaders and builders should audit their emotional reactions to AI errors versus human errors. If you wouldn't fire a teammate for a misunderstanding, don't write off your AI tool for the same.
source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Trust and charitable interpretation are foundational to effective collaboration, whether with humans or AI. Jeremy extends grace to a brilliant friend who misunderstood a request because he believes in his competence and good intentions — the same orientation is required for productive AI use.
original_quote: When I interact with a human teammate, I start from a fundamental belief that they're thoughtful, capable, and trying their best. If they miss something, I give them the benefit of the doubt. I clarify. We iterate.
implication: Approach AI with the same presumption of competence you extend to trusted colleagues. Charitable interpretation unlocks iteration and improvement rather than premature abandonment.
