source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: AI performs to the level of expectations you set for it — not because it is literally responsive to belief, but because low expectations produce lazy prompts, which produce mediocre outputs. This creates a self-fulfilling prophecy of AI underperformance.
original_quote: If you don't think it can do something, it probably won't. Not because it can't, but because your low expectations produce lazy prompts. Lazy prompts produce mediocre outputs. And then you go, 'See? I knew AI couldn't do that.' Self-fulfilling prophecy.
implication: Raising your expectations of AI is not magical thinking — it is a forcing function for writing better prompts. Builders should assume AI capability and design their prompts accordingly.
source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: When AI output disappoints, the default assumption should be misunderstanding rather than incapability. This reframe shifts the response from frustration to clarification — the same productive instinct we deploy with human colleagues.
original_quote: When it doesn't nail something, assume it misunderstood—not that it can't. That's what I did with my whip-smart collaborator because that's what you do with teammates you trust. You don't immediately conclude they're useless. You clarify. You give them another shot. You provide more context.
implication: Treating AI underperformance as a communication problem rather than a capability problem keeps the collaboration alive and productive. Add context, reframe, and try again before giving up.
source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Interacting with AI via voice rather than typing fundamentally changes the collaborative dynamic. We naturally interact with human teammates through speech, and applying that same modality to AI unlocks a more fluid, conversational, and productive relationship.
original_quote: We interact with teammates via our voice, not our thumbs. If you're still typing everything, you're barely scratching the surface. The collaborative dynamic changes the second you start speaking to it.
implication: Switching to voice interaction with AI is a low-effort, high-leverage habit change. Leaders should experiment with spoken prompts to experience a qualitatively different — and richer — collaboration.
source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Inviting AI to ask clarifying questions before responding transforms an interaction from a one-way command into a genuine dialogue. The questions AI asks often surface hidden assumptions in the requester's own thinking, leading to significantly better outcomes.
original_quote: Before providing an answer, please ask me any questions you need to better understand my request and deliver spectacular results. This simple addition transforms the interaction from a one-way command to a collaborative dialogue. The questions the AI asks will often reveal assumptions you didn't realize you were making.
implication: A single sentence added to any prompt — asking AI to question before answering — can dramatically improve output quality and reveal blind spots in your own framing of a problem.
source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Naming AI assistants — giving them personas like 'Betty Budget' or 'Aiden Adman' — changes the behavior of the human interacting with them, not the AI itself. Named personas prompt users to provide more context, explain needs more clearly, and engage in genuine dialogue.
original_quote: When I'm interacting with 'Betty' versus 'Budget Analysis Tool,' I naturally provide more context, explain my needs more clearly, and engage in more of a dialogue. The results speak for themselves.
implication: Humanizing AI through naming is a behavioral hack that improves prompt quality without requiring any technical skill. Teams should experiment with persona-named GPTs for recurring workflows.
source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: AI's speed creates a new bottleneck: human capacity to review and metabolize output. When AI produces in 20 minutes what previously took two weeks, the human's ability to process and act on that information becomes the constraint — not the generation itself.
original_quote: AI does in 20 minutes what used to take your team two weeks. Great, right? Except here's the problem: you're not ready to review two weeks of work in 20 minutes. Your ability to metabolize the information is now the bottleneck.
implication: Builders and leaders must redesign their workflows to include deliberate review time for AI-generated work. Blocking calendar time for review is now as important as blocking time for generation.
source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Onboarding AI the same way you would onboard a new human teammate — with examples, style guides, feedback on drafts, and context about stakeholder preferences — dramatically improves its output quality. Most people skip this step and then blame AI for underperforming.
original_quote: If you had a new teammate who could write your all-staff memos, how would you onboard them? You wouldn't just say 'write me a memo' and expect perfection, right? You'd show them examples. Give them your company's style guide. Provide feedback on drafts. Have you onboarded AI like that?
implication: Organizations should develop structured AI onboarding protocols — examples, brand guidelines, past work, stakeholder context — treating AI setup as a talent onboarding investment rather than a one-click tool deployment.
source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Treating AI as a tool rather than a teammate leaves massive capability unrealized — not because the AI lacks capability, but because the tool framing prevents users from setting it up to succeed. The orientation shapes the outcome.
original_quote: Every day you treat AI like a tool instead of a teammate, you're leaving massive capability on the table. Not because AI can't do more—but because you're not setting it up to succeed.
implication: The most important AI upgrade many organizations can make is not a new model or feature — it is a shift in the mental model from 'tool' to 'teammate.' This reframe changes how people prompt, onboard, and iterate.
source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Poor AI outcomes are frequently the user's fault, not the AI's. Low expectations lead to under-specified prompts, which predictably produce weak outputs — a dynamic that is then misattributed to AI's limitations rather than the quality of the human's input.
original_quote: The truth? You failed to expect enough to prompt well enough to give it a fair shot.
implication: Before concluding AI cannot do something, creators and leaders should examine whether they articulated the task with sufficient clarity, context, and ambition. Prompt quality is a learnable, improvable skill.
source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: The reflexive frustration most people feel when AI errs — compared to the grace they extend to human colleagues for identical errors — reveals an underlying orientation to AI as inferior rather than as a capable partner. This orientation, not AI's capability, is the limiting factor.
original_quote: AI gets none of that grace. And that reveals everything about how we're actually orienting to it.
implication: Improving AI collaboration is as much an emotional and attitudinal challenge as a technical one. Leaders should reflect on the emotional assumptions they bring to AI interactions and work to align them with how they treat trusted human colleagues.
source_ref: If You Were an AI, I'd Think You Were Terrible — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: Jeremy Utley holds a lecturer position at Stanford, situating him within one of the world's leading institutions for design thinking and innovation education. This academic grounding gives his work on creativity and AI a rigorous, research-informed foundation.
implication: Practitioners engaging with Jeremy's frameworks can expect them to be rooted in Stanford's design thinking tradition, making them more transferable to organizational and educational contexts.
source_ref: Beyond the Prompt — Meet your hosts (Jeremy Utley & Henrik Werdelin)

content: Jeremy Utley is the author of Ideaflow, a book that signals his core intellectual focus: the systematic generation and management of ideas as a measurable, improvable process. The title itself frames creativity not as a mysterious gift but as a flow that can be cultivated.
implication: Leaders and creators should treat idea generation as a practice with throughput metrics, not a random event — suggesting processes and habits matter more than innate talent.
source_ref: Beyond the Prompt — Meet your hosts (Jeremy Utley & Henrik Werdelin)

content: Jeremy has delivered a talk on AI and creativity that has been watched by over 3 million people, making it a viral piece of thought leadership. This reach indicates he has become a prominent public voice at the intersection of artificial intelligence and human creative potential.
implication: There is massive public appetite for practical guidance on how AI intersects with creativity — builders and communicators who address this intersection clearly can achieve outsized reach.
source_ref: Beyond the Prompt — Meet your hosts (Jeremy Utley & Henrik Werdelin)

content: Jeremy operates across multiple roles simultaneously — Stanford lecturer, author, and AI keynote speaker — demonstrating a portfolio approach to intellectual work. This multi-role identity allows him to translate academic insight into practitioner-facing content at scale.
implication: Thought leaders who bridge academia, publishing, and public speaking can amplify their ideas far beyond what any single channel allows — a model worth considering for anyone building influence around a core idea.
source_ref: Beyond the Prompt — Meet your hosts (Jeremy Utley & Henrik Werdelin)

content: Jeremy co-hosts the podcast 'Beyond the Prompt' alongside Henrik Werdelin, a serial founder and investor. The pairing deliberately combines an academic creativity expert with a hands-on entrepreneur, suggesting the show frames AI not just theoretically but through a builder's lens.
implication: Combining creative theory with startup pragmatism is a differentiating format — audiences get frameworks they can immediately apply, not just abstract concepts.
source_ref: Beyond the Prompt — Meet your hosts (Jeremy Utley & Henrik Werdelin)

content: Jeremy is identified as an 'AI keynote speaker,' indicating that organizations are actively seeking his perspective on AI's implications for creativity and work. This positions him as a guide for institutions navigating the creative disruptions brought by AI.
implication: Organizations uncertain about AI's impact on creative work are looking for trusted translators — those who can synthesize research and practice into actionable direction hold significant influence right now.
source_ref: Beyond the Prompt — Meet your hosts (Jeremy Utley & Henrik Werdelin)

content: The framing of Jeremy as 'the voice behind a viral talk on AI and creativity' suggests his communication style — accessible, compelling, and broadly resonant — is a core part of his value proposition, not just his subject matter expertise.
implication: In a crowded information landscape, how you communicate an idea matters as much as the idea itself. Clarity and narrative craft are competitive advantages for anyone working in the ideas economy.
source_ref: Beyond the Prompt — Meet your hosts (Jeremy Utley & Henrik Werdelin)

content: Jeremy's book is titled 'Ideaflow,' a concept that reframes creativity as something with velocity and volume — a flow state of ideas rather than isolated flashes of insight. This framing implies that more ideas, generated more consistently, leads to better creative outcomes.
implication: Builders and innovators should focus on increasing their rate of idea generation as a primary lever for creative quality — optimizing for quantity and consistency before filtering for quality.
source_ref: Beyond the Prompt — Meet your hosts (Jeremy Utley & Henrik Werdelin)

content: Jeremy Utley positions AI not as a tool to be used but as a collaborator to work with. The distinction matters because the word 'use' frames AI as passive and instrumental, while 'work with' implies partnership, iteration, and co-creation. This reframe changes how leaders engage with AI and the results they get from it.
original_quote: Don't Use AI. Work With It.
implication: Leaders who treat AI as a vending machine will get vending-machine results. Shifting to a collaborative mindset unlocks AI's capacity to amplify creativity and judgment rather than just automate tasks.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: Most people interact with AI episodically, reintroducing themselves and their context each session, which severely limits what AI can do for them. Utley models a more advanced approach — treating AI as a persistent 'chief of staff' that holds accumulated context and can execute complex tasks autonomously. He describes having his AI chief of staff read 50 pre-event transcripts and generate an FAQ in three minutes.
original_quote: I asked my AI chief of staff to read 50 pre-event transcripts and write the FAQ I'd been avoiding for months. It took three minutes.
implication: The leverage gap between casual AI users and power users is not about technical skill — it's about whether you've invested in giving AI persistent, rich context. Leaders who do this unlock qualitatively different output.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: There are dramatically different levels of AI augmentation, and most people are operating at the lowest tier. Utley contrasts drafting emails with running 27 parallel AI agents on a single problem from a mobile device with full laptop context. These are not incremental differences — they represent fundamentally different relationships with AI capability.
original_quote: There are levels of AI augmentation: Some people use AI to draft emails. Josh used it to run 27 agents on a single problem, from his mobile on the road, with all the context of his laptop at home. Those are not the same thing.
implication: Organizations benchmarking AI adoption by surface-level usage metrics are missing the point. The real question is whether people are operating at the frontier of what AI augmentation makes possible.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: Utley argues that a real concern about AI is not the same as a valid conclusion. Using AI sycophancy as an example, he distinguishes between people who raise concerns as off-ramps — reasons to disengage — and those who convert concerns into curriculum and learning opportunities. The difference determines whether someone grows with AI or stagnates.
original_quote: A real concern is not a valid conclusion. An EVP in finance emailed me last week about AI sycophancy. Most people raise that concern as an off-ramp. He made it the curriculum.
implication: Leaders must audit their own relationship to AI concerns — are they using legitimate worries as permission to avoid learning, or as prompts to go deeper? The latter posture is what separates early adopters from perpetual skeptics.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: Utley identifies two distinct fears operating in the AI era: one that is fuel for action and one that is self-fulfilling paralysis. He frames the difference through neuroscience, suggesting that understanding which fear is driving you changes how you approach every AI conversation. Being 'not behind enough to panic' but 'too afraid to learn' is a particularly dangerous middle ground.
original_quote: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.
implication: The most at-risk professionals in the AI transition are not those who know nothing — they are those who know just enough to feel okay but are too anxious to actively experiment. Identifying which fear is operating is the first intervention.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: AI fluency is a learnable skill, not a cognitive shortcut or personality trait. Utley cites a 40% quality boost available to anyone who develops the skill, yet fewer than 3% of the workforce has taken it up — and that number is declining. He made this observation while teaching at Wharton, suggesting the gap is motivational and cultural, not technical.
original_quote: A 40% quality boost, available to anyone. Taken up by fewer than 3% of the workforce (and that number is going down, not up).
implication: The AI skills gap is not primarily a training-content problem — if it were, adoption would be rising. Something else is suppressing uptake. Leaders need to diagnose and address the cultural and psychological barriers, not just provide more resources.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: Standard AI dashboards measure visible activity — prompt experiments, tool logins — but miss the most meaningful transformations. Utley highlights the example of a finance manager who quietly eliminated three recurring reports, creating real organizational change that never registers on usage metrics. What gets measured shapes what gets valued.
original_quote: The engineer who experiments with 40 prompts shows up on the dashboard. The finance manager who quietly kills three recurring reports doesn't — even though she's the one who actually changed.
implication: Organizations need to rethink how they measure AI impact. Activity metrics reward visible experimentation but ignore the quieter, higher-leverage work of redesigning workflows and eliminating unnecessary processes. Better measurement frameworks are needed.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: As AI takes over drafting, building, and prototyping, the professional's core contribution is shifting from authorship to stewardship. This framing — introduced via a guest post from Brice Challamel, Head of AI Strategy at OpenAI — argues the contribution isn't diminished but transformed: from making things to curating, directing, and taking responsibility for what AI produces.
original_quote: If AI can draft the memo, build the deck, and prototype the product, what exactly is the professional's contribution? Brice says it's not 'less' — it's 'different.' Read his case for the shift from authorship to stewardship.
implication: Professionals who cling to authorship as their value proposition will feel threatened by AI. Those who embrace stewardship — owning the judgment, direction, and accountability behind AI output — will find their roles elevated rather than eroded.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: The answer to nearly every 'could AI do this?' question is yes — the bottleneck is that most people haven't yet formed the question. Utley illustrates this with NBA legend Manu Ginobili raising his hand during a Spurs AI session: the moment someone asks 'could AI...?' is the moment real value creation begins.
original_quote: When Manu Ginobili raised his hand during a Spurs AI session and said 'Could AI...?' — that was the ball game. The answer to almost every 'could it' question is yes. The problem is most people don't have a question yet.
implication: The constraint on AI value is not capability — it's question formation. Leaders should invest in developing the habit of asking 'could AI do this?' across every domain, because the capability almost certainly exists.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: Utley has spent over 20 years equipping leaders to innovate and has taught 4 million+ students worldwide. His frameworks draw from nearly two decades of teaching at Stanford, and his current courses are available through Stanford Online and Harvard Executive Education. His work bridges academic innovation theory and practical AI application for non-technical leaders.
original_quote: Jeremy has guided the world's most innovative entrepreneurs, corporations, and government leaders. Jeremy's frameworks are drawn from nearly two decades of teaching at Stanford.
implication: Utley's credibility is built on rare combination of long-tenure academic rigor and real-world practitioner results. For leaders seeking AI guidance, this signals frameworks that are both theoretically grounded and operationally tested.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: Utley's AI work produces measurable, concrete outcomes, not just mindset shifts. His interventions have saved 7,000+ labor days for National Park Service rangers and helped reinvent corporate workflows at Qualtrics. These examples demonstrate that AI adoption, done right, creates quantifiable operational value.
original_quote: From saving 7,000+ labor days for National Park Service rangers to reinventing corporate workflows at Qualtrics, he turns complex AI ideas into actionable results.
implication: AI transformation should be evaluated against tangible outcomes — labor hours recovered, processes eliminated, decisions accelerated. Utley's case studies provide a model for how to frame and measure AI's organizational ROI.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: Utley's viral video — watched by over 3 million people — centers on a single mindset shift that determines whether AI amplifies your creativity or merely automates your output. He argues most leaders fundamentally misunderstand where AI's value lies, and that accessing its true potential requires changing how you think, not just how you prompt.
original_quote: AI expert Jeremy Utley explains why most leaders miss AI's real value. This 2-minute video reveals the single mindset shift that lets AI amplify creativity, drive results, and make you irreplaceable.
implication: Tool adoption without mindset change produces marginal gains. The leaders who become irreplaceable in an AI-saturated world are those who understand how to direct AI toward creative and strategic ends — not just efficiency.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: Utley publishes a weekly newsletter called 'Methods of the Masters,' reaching 33,147+ leaders and creators, paired with a free bonus chapter from his book Ideaflow on thinking like Bezos and Jobs. This suggests his intellectual framework connects AI adoption to broader creative and innovation principles drawn from history's most generative thinkers.
original_quote: Join 33,147+ leaders and creators exploring AI, innovation, and strategy each week with Methods of the Masters, plus get a free bonus chapter from Ideaflow: 'How to Think Like Bezos and Jobs.'
implication: Utley situates AI within a longer tradition of creative mastery — suggesting the skills that made great innovators are the same ones that unlock AI's value today. Learning from masters isn't just historical interest; it's practical preparation.
source_ref: JeremyUtley.com — Best AI Keynote Speaker

content: Creativity is not a rare gift reserved for a select few — it is an inherent human quality that everyone possesses. Like any skill or muscle, it can be developed, strengthened, and refined through deliberate practice. The difference between 'creative' and 'uncreative' people is simply practice, not innate talent.
original_quote: Creativity is an inherent human quality, which everyone shares. But some people practice using their creative faculties, while others do not. It's just like any other skill or muscle. It can be developed, strengthened, and refined through practice.
implication: Leaders and individuals should stop self-selecting out of creative work by believing they 'aren't creative.' Treating creativity as a trainable skill opens the door to deliberate development programs and personal creative habits.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: A seventh-grader's definition — 'Creativity is doing more than the first thing you think of' — is both brilliantly simple and cognitively accurate. Humans have a well-documented 'first idea bias' (also called functional fixedness or cognitive closure) where they stop thinking once a solution appears. Research shows that among 10 possible solutions, the first is very unlikely to be the best.
original_quote: Creativity is doing more than the first thing you think of... There's a well-documented human tendency called the 'first idea bias,' or what some refer to as 'functional fixedness' or 'cognitive closure.' It means that, once we come up with some sort of solution, we often stop thinking. Research shows that, if you think of 10 different solutions, the first is very unlikely to be the best.
implication: Builders and creative teams should institutionalize the habit of generating multiple alternatives before evaluating any solution. Stopping at the first idea is a cognitive trap, not a creative endpoint.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: AI is uniquely well-suited to overcome first idea bias because it can generate alternatives effortlessly and at scale using analogy, lateral thinking, and combinatorial association. Unlike humans, for whom ideation consumes cognitive energy, AI can keep producing ideas without fatigue. This makes AI a powerful tool for expanding the idea space rather than just reaching 'good enough' faster.
original_quote: Unlike humans, for whom ideation consumes energy, AI can generate alternatives effortlessly and quickly using analogy, lateral thinking, or combinatorial association. That's creative by definition.
implication: Teams should use AI not just for efficiency but as a divergence engine — explicitly prompting it for 10, 20, or 50 alternatives to avoid premature convergence on a merely adequate idea.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: AI's potential to enhance human creativity is largely unrealized in practice because people use it to arrive at 'good enough' ideas quickly rather than pushing further. This is a human problem, not a technology problem — cognitive biases like first idea fixation limit how deeply people explore with AI. True creative potential is only unlocked when the human consciously pushes the model beyond the first acceptable answer.
original_quote: AI often helps people get to 'good enough' ideas very quickly. But that's more about efficiency than creativity. Unless the human consciously pushes the model for more, they won't unlock true creativity.
implication: Organizations measuring AI's creative ROI should track not just speed of output but depth of exploration. Training people to push past 'good enough' responses is as important as training them to prompt at all.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: AI functions like a mirror — it reflects and amplifies what the user brings to it. An expert who approaches the model with deep domain knowledge gets expert-level responses; a novice gets far less valuable output. The model doesn't replace your creativity; it extends and amplifies it based on the quality of thinking you contribute.
original_quote: The AI model reflects what you bring to it. If you're an HR expert, and you approach the model with expert-level thinking, you'll get an expert response... AI reveals things to you it won't reveal to someone else. That's why I say the model's power is in its ability to match and extend the user's thinking. It doesn't replace your creativity, it amplifies it.
implication: Investing in your own domain expertise and thinking quality is still essential in the AI age — perhaps more so. The ceiling of what AI can do for you is set by the quality of what you bring to the conversation.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: Treating AI as a teammate rather than a tool is a mindset shift with practical consequences for output quality. The way you engage with AI — your tone, curiosity, and respect — directly influences the quality of what you receive back. Utley argues that even small gestures like saying please and thank you matter less for the AI's benefit and more for maintaining your own humanity and quality of presence.
original_quote: People sometimes ask me, 'Should I say please and thank you to AI?' My answer is: What does it cost your humanity if you don't?... Bring your full self, your curiosity, your respect. That's the teammate approach. And it gets better results.
implication: Leaders should frame AI adoption as a relational shift, not just a tooling upgrade. Teams that approach AI with genuine engagement and curiosity will consistently outperform those who treat it as a vending machine.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: The most common mistake people make with AI is providing insufficient context — often just a few words, equivalent to a Google search. Utley recommends never prompting a model without at least 400 words of context, treating the interaction like onboarding a new team member. People give more context to interns than to AI, then blame the AI for poor outputs.
original_quote: I would recommend never prompting a model without at least 400 words of context. Most people say, 'My average prompt is five words.' Well, that's a Google search... We give more context to interns than we do to AI. That's the mistake.
implication: Organizations should train teams to front-load context — brand voice, objectives, constraints, audience — before making any substantive AI request. Prompt quality is a learnable skill that directly governs output quality.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: A powerful onboarding exercise for AI adoption is asking the model to play the role of a 'ChatGPT adviser' and interview you about your workflows, responsibilities, and KPIs before making recommendations. This 'double delivery' prompt both surfaces immediate use cases and teaches you how to partner with AI more deeply over time. Unlike Excel or PowerPoint, AI can teach you how to use it.
original_quote: Unlike tools such as Excel or PowerPoint, which can't teach you how to use them, AI can teach you how to use AI. That paradigm shifts how you think about learning... I like to call it a 'double delivery' exercise. It delivers on your current objective, and it opens the door to deeper exploration.
implication: Companies rolling out AI tools should start with this self-interview prompt as an onboarding ritual — it is role-independent, scalable, and generates personalized recommendations that immediately build confidence and practical fluency.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: In creative workflows, posing multiple questions — not just one — is fundamental to generating breadth of ideas. If you ask only one question and it's the wrong one, you're stuck. Asking more than one minimizes the risk of solving the wrong problem and prevents premature evaluation by keeping the option space open longer.
original_quote: One of the most important things is to pose more than one question. Seek more than one answer. Try more than one thing... Often, people are seeking to address the wrong problem. If you ask only one question, and it's the wrong one, you're stuck.
implication: Innovators and product teams should build in a 'question multiplication' step before any ideation session — generating 5–10 problem framings before locking in on one. AI makes this cheap and fast to execute.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: AI can function as a creativity coach by taking on specific personas — such as a creativity expert or design strategist — and providing meaningful feedback that pushes thinking and generates alternative suggestions. This is not fundamentally different from what a human coach or consultant would do; the key is knowing to ask the right questions and assign the right role.
original_quote: If you give an AI model a specific persona – a creativity expert, say – and then ask it to evaluate an idea, it can provide meaningful feedback that pushes your thinking, as well as generate alternative suggestions. That's not fundamentally different from what we might expect from, say, a negotiation coach or a design strategist.
implication: Individuals and teams without access to expensive coaches or consultants can use persona-assigned AI prompts to get substantive creative and strategic feedback on demand — democratizing access to high-quality creative mentorship.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: The future of ideation will feature AI as an equal partner sitting at the creative table alongside human teams. Utley envisions AI becoming a trusted creative colleague — one that always has new ideas, pushes thinking, and helps teams see blind spots. This represents a fundamental shift in how creative teams are composed and how ideas are generated.
original_quote: I cannot imagine a world where AI is not an equal partner in the ideation process. It's going to sit at the table as a member of creative teams. Teams will come to see it as a creative partner, a trusted colleague who always has new ideas, who pushes your thinking, who helps you see blind spots.
implication: Leaders should begin designing team structures and creative processes now with AI as a standing participant — not an occasional tool. This includes defining how AI inputs are weighted, reviewed, and integrated into decision-making.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: The fundamental nature of human creativity and the creative impulse has not changed over the 15 years Utley has been teaching, despite new tools and interfaces. What has changed is the context of uncertainty — today's students face a future where they cannot even identify the range of jobs that will exist, making career planning a form of creative planning under uncertainty.
original_quote: Humans are humans. There may be new tools, new interfaces, but, fundamentally, the creative impulse is the same... Students say things like, 'I don't even know what jobs are going to be there.' That fundamental uncertainty is new... The challenge now isn't just career planning. It's creative planning under uncertainty.
implication: Education and leadership development programs should shift from teaching fixed career frameworks to teaching adaptive creative thinking — the ability to generate options and navigate uncertainty is the core competency for the AI era.
source_ref: Creativity in the age of AI: A conversation with Jeremy Utley — Capgemini

content: Ideaflow is the rate at which you or your team can generate ideas in a set amount of time, and Utley argues it is the only business metric that truly matters. Innovation is not a single event, sprint, or hackathon — it is a continuous discipline. Mastering ideaflow elevates every other capability a person or organization has.
original_quote: Innovation is not a one-time event, nor is it a sprint or a hackathon. It comes from the mastery of ideaflow, a technique that advances everything else you do.
implication: Leaders and builders should measure and optimize their idea-generation rate, not just output quality. Treating ideaflow as a trackable metric reframes creativity as a manageable, improvable process.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: Innovators focus on inputs rather than outputs, and they prioritize quantity of ideas over quality. The obsession with producing one great idea is a trap — generating many ideas is the actual engine of breakthrough thinking. Volume creates the conditions from which quality eventually emerges.
original_quote: Innovators focus on inputs instead of outputs. And innovators don't obsess over quality, they develop quantity.
implication: Creators and teams should resist the urge to evaluate ideas too early. Building a high-volume idea practice — without premature filtering — is the foundational habit of consistently innovative people.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: Every problem, at its core, is an ideas problem. Whether in Fortune 50 companies, mid-market firms, or startups, teams succeed to the degree that ideas flow freely. Constraining or blocking ideaflow is therefore a direct constraint on organizational performance.
original_quote: Every problem is an ideas problem. Having worked in Fortune 50 companies, mid-market companies, and startups, I have seen that teams succeed to the degree that there is a free flow of ideas.
implication: Leaders should diagnose organizational problems not just as resource or execution failures, but as idea-flow failures. Removing barriers to free ideation is a high-leverage intervention for any struggling team.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: Inspiration is not a passive, mystical experience — it is the disciplined pursuit of unexpected input. Utley reframes 'waiting for inspiration' as an active, structured practice of seeking out novel stimuli. This redefines inspiration as a skill that can be cultivated, not a gift that arrives randomly.
original_quote: When we say inspiration, we mean the disciplined pursuit of unexpected input. That's what we mean by inspiration.
implication: Creators should build systematic routines for consuming diverse, unexpected inputs — outside their domain, comfort zone, or industry. Inspiration is not luck; it is the output of a deliberate input strategy.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: The standard four-step model of creativity — preparation, incubation, illumination, verification — is widely acknowledged, but the incubation stage is severely undernourished in modern work culture. The always-on, always-productive orientation of today's workplaces starves the very stage where ideas gestate. Efficiency obsession actively undermines creative effectiveness.
original_quote: Incubation is severely undernourished in today's always-connected, always-productive, always-efficient environment. And our efficiency orientation often keeps us from being effective.
implication: Individuals and organizations must deliberately protect unstructured time. Without incubation space built into schedules, the illumination stage — the breakthrough moment — rarely arrives on demand.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: Carl Liebert, former CEO of Keller Williams, blocks off every Friday to think broadly, seek inspiration, and explore interesting ideas — refusing all meetings that day. Utley uses this as a model for how leaders can weaponize their calendars to protect creative capacity. Most people are victims of their calendars; creative people use their calendars as weapons.
original_quote: What creative people do is wield their calendar as a weapon. That's what Carl's done, he's actually wielded it to protect his ability to think broadly.
implication: Senior leaders especially must treat calendar architecture as a creative strategy, not just a logistics tool. Blocking protected thinking time — and defending it rigorously — is a hallmark of high-performing, inventive leaders.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: Jeff Bezos in Amazon's early days reserved Mondays and Thursdays with no standing meetings, spending the time scrolling the internet, examining his own website, and walking the halls. Utley cites this as evidence that wildly inventive people deliberately block time to think — a practice that looks unproductive but drives outsized innovation.
original_quote: In the early days of Amazon, Jeff Bezos spent, I think it was Mondays and Thursdays, and he didn't have any standing meetings. And he would just scroll the internet for hours... But the point is wildly inventive people block time to think. It sounds obvious.
implication: The behaviors that produce major innovations often look like idle wandering to outsiders. Building legitimacy for 'thinking time' within organizations — and modeling it at the top — is a structural innovation advantage.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: Lin-Manuel Miranda's wife observed that his best ideas — including the concept for Hamilton — came during vacation when he was relaxed and removed from routine. She advised him to 'book a weekly vacation,' leading to a practice where he extends family trips by staying an extra week alone for creative incubation. Utley uses this story to show that peak creative output often requires radical disconnection from normal work.
original_quote: His wife observed, you had your very best idea when we were on vacation. You were laying on a pool float, drinking a margarita. And you came up with this idea of a rap opera based on the life of the founding father of America. She said you know what we need to do. You've got to book a weekly vacation.
implication: The conditions that produce breakthrough ideas are often the opposite of the conditions in which most people work. Designing recurring retreat-like spaces — even brief ones — can unlock creative output that normal work environments suppress.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: Creative problem-solving requires space — psychological and temporal — to function effectively. Without space, the incubation stage of the creative process is bypassed, and people jump from preparation directly to forced output. Space is not a luxury for creative work; it is a structural requirement.
original_quote: Creativity and problem solving, more broadly, needs space.
implication: Teams and individuals should audit how much unstructured, low-pressure time exists in their work rhythms. If every hour is scheduled and accountable, creative breakthroughs become statistically unlikely regardless of talent or effort.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: There are moments that call for expansive, divergent thinking ('flare') and moments that require convergent focus. Knowing which mode is appropriate — and being able to shift between them — is a core creative competency. Conflating the two modes undermines both ideation and execution.
original_quote: There's times we need to flare, and there's times we need to focus.
implication: Facilitators, leaders, and individual creators should explicitly signal which mode is active at any given moment. Mixing evaluation with generation kills idea quantity; mixing divergence with deadline execution kills follow-through.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: One of the most significant inhibitors to creative efficacy is simply the lack of time — people feel they are 'victims of the calendar.' Utley argues this is a choice, not a circumstance. High-output creative individuals actively reclaim time by designing their schedules around thinking, not just doing.
original_quote: We're victims of the calendar. But what creative people do is wield their calendar as a weapon.
implication: The calendar is the most underrated creative tool available to any knowledge worker. Reclaiming even one recurring block per week for unstructured thinking can meaningfully shift creative output over time.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: Utley serves as Director of Executive Education at Stanford's d.school and co-hosts 'Stanford's Masters of Creativity,' positioning the d.school as a place where creative problem-solving is taught as a rigorous, learnable discipline. This institutional context grounds his frameworks in both academic research and executive practice.
implication: Leaders seeking to build creative cultures can draw on validated, research-backed frameworks from design thinking traditions rather than treating creativity as purely innate. Creativity can be systematically taught and operationalized.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck

content: Design thinking is fundamentally a language for interdisciplinary collaboration that puts the human being at the center of creativity and innovation efforts. It aligns teams not just on solutions but — more importantly — on the human problem to be solved. All expertise is then placed in service of that human being.
original_quote: Design thinking, very simply, it's a language for interdisciplinary collaboration. So you've got folks coming from different backgrounds. How do you make sure to maximize the respective perspectives and expertise of a diverse group? Design thinking offers one such way. It really puts the human being whose life you're trying to improve at the center of your creativity and innovation efforts.
implication: Leaders building cross-functional teams should invest in shared problem-framing language before jumping to solutions — alignment on the problem is the prerequisite for genuine innovation.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: Most people and organizations default heavily toward convergent thinking — making decisions, eliminating options, reducing variability. But divergent thinking, which generates options and embraces variation, is equally critical to innovation and far less practiced. The imbalance stems from education, organizational incentives, and ingrained norms.
original_quote: The challenge is recognizing that diverging, generating options, embracing variation is also critical to the innovation process and the creative process. And yet we're much less practiced at that. And because of practice, because of our education, because of our organizational incentives and structures and norms, we're much more comfortable converging.
implication: Organizations need to deliberately create protected space and explicit permission for divergent thinking — it won't happen naturally because every structural force pushes people toward premature convergence.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: A useful meta-language for creative work is simply asking: are we diverging or converging right now? This simple distinction changes how participants should show up — converging calls for critical judgment, while diverging calls for generative association. Knowing which mode you're in prevents the common mistake of applying the wrong mindset at the wrong moment.
original_quote: One way, one very simple kind of language we'll use is just, are we focusing or are we flaring? Are we converging? Are we diverging? And that helps me know who I should be when I show up. If I'm converging, simple example, I'm going to be more critical. I'm going to be more judgmental... If I'm diverging from trying to generate possibilities, I'm not going to say, what do I think of this idea? I'm going to say, what does this idea make me think of?
implication: Facilitators and team leads should explicitly name the mode of thinking at the start of any creative session — this single habit prevents the idea-killing dynamic of premature evaluation.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: Problem framing is itself a valuable creative output, not just a precursor to solutions. Utley cites John Dewey's principle that 'a problem well put is half solved,' arguing that the articulation of the right problem is a non-trivial and meaningful stepping stone toward innovation. When the problem is properly framed, the solution almost handles itself.
original_quote: John Dewey, who's an American educational philosopher, he said, a problem well put is half solved. And a lot of times what we find is when you frame the problem properly, the solution almost handles itself. And so much of the innovation actually in the design thinking approach writ large is legitimizing the problem as a meaningful output of a design effort.
implication: Teams should budget significant time and energy for problem definition and treat a well-articulated problem statement as a deliverable worth celebrating — not a box to rush through on the way to solutions.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: Innovation has become over-hyped as a topic while remaining an under-nourished capability in practice. This gap exists not just at the individual level but also at the team and organizational levels. Having taught innovation for 14+ years at Stanford, Utley sees this pattern consistently across industries and geographies.
original_quote: Having taught the subject of innovation for the last 14+ years at Stanford, he has seen the topic become over-hyped, yet remain an under-nourished capability. This is true not only on the personal level, but also on the team and organizational levels.
implication: Organizations that talk about innovation loudly but don't build the underlying daily practices and metrics to support it are engaged in performance, not transformation. The focus should shift from innovation theater to measurable creative capability-building.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: Ideaflow is presented as the only business metric that matters for innovation — a measurable rate at which an individual, team, or organization generates ideas. Rather than treating creativity as a mysterious event, Ideaflow reframes it as a measurable, manageable flow that can be tracked and improved systematically.
original_quote: Besides the name of award winning groundbreaking new book by professors from Stanford, the premise of IdeaFlow is simple.
implication: Leaders seeking to improve innovation outcomes should start by measuring idea volume before optimizing for idea quality — you can't manage what you don't measure, and most organizations have no baseline for their creative output.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: Utley describes himself as 'a recovering MBA' whose life was 'pleasantly derailed' by design thinking while working at a startup in India. This origin story illustrates that exposure to human-centered problem solving can fundamentally reorient someone trained in analytical, convergent frameworks — the shift is available to anyone.
original_quote: My life was derailed by the design school at Stanford about over 15 years ago. I was actually working at a startup in India doing my MBA stuff. I'm a recovering MBA. So I love a good spreadsheet, love a good pivot table. And while I was working for this startup in India, my life was like, say pleasantly derailed by the practice of design thinking.
implication: You don't need a creative background to become a creative practitioner — analytical thinkers who encounter design thinking often find it powerfully complementary to, not in conflict with, their existing skills.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: Utley frames his role not as an expert dispensing answers but as 'a front row student in the world's coolest classroom.' This learning posture — even after 14+ years teaching at Stanford — reflects a belief that creative mastery is a continuous becoming, not a fixed state to be achieved.
original_quote: He considers himself to be a 'front row student in the world's coolest classroom,' and shines a spotlight on the ways every single one of us can emulate creative masters and grow in our own journey of becoming.
implication: Leaders who model intellectual humility and genuine curiosity create the psychological safety that teams need to take creative risks — positioning yourself as a perpetual learner is itself a leadership practice.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: Utley's current research focuses on the impact of generative AI on problem solving and innovation inside large organizations. This positions AI not as a consumer tool but as a structural force reshaping how organizations generate and process ideas at scale.
original_quote: Jeremy Utley's current research concerns the impact of generative AI on problem solving and innovation inside large organizations.
implication: Organizations need to proactively study how AI is changing their internal innovation processes — the question is no longer whether AI affects creativity at scale, but how leaders should design workflows, roles, and culture in response.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: Utley emphasizes that the practices fueling breakthrough thinking are personal and interpersonal — not events or one-off workshops. Sustained creative output is the result of ongoing habits and relational dynamics, not isolated brainstorming sessions or innovation sprints.
original_quote: In this searching examination of the personal and interpersonal practices (rather than events) that fuel breakthrough thinking, Jeremy Utley shares insights from his bestselling book.
implication: Organizations that rely on periodic innovation events — hackathons, offsites, brainstorms — without building the underlying daily practices will see their creative output plateau. Lasting ideaflow requires behavioral infrastructure, not just scheduled moments.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: Utley notes that design thinking and the concept of IdeaFlow stand on the shoulders of giants — they inherit from a lineage including David Kelley, Stanford's product design tradition, anthropology, and many sedimentary layers of prior thinking. No transformative framework is truly original; it synthesizes and builds on what came before.
original_quote: We stand on the shoulders of giants. And one of the things that's really been astounding to us is to see from our perspective at Stanford... We did, we stand on the shoulders of giants of. Not just David Kelly, who's our direct founder, but also a lineage of product design in Stanford and a lineage of anthropology and building to think.
implication: Innovators and creators should invest heavily in studying the history of their field — deep knowledge of prior work is not a constraint on originality but a prerequisite for genuine synthesis and advance.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: Creativity and the capacity for innovative ideas is universal — not a trait reserved for artists or designated 'creative' roles. Utley's framing, echoed in the podcast's opening, is that every person is creative, and this capacity has never been more important in work and beyond.
original_quote: It doesn't matter who you are or what you do. You're creative. We all are. And the capacity for innovative and creative ideas has never been more important in our work lives and beyond.
implication: Leaders should stop segmenting teams into 'creative' and 'non-creative' functions and instead build systems and cultures that activate the creative capacity that already exists in every person on their team.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders

content: Jeremy Utley argues that 'ideaflow' is the only business metric that truly matters. The core premise is that organizations and individuals must proactively exercise their creative muscles so that the very best ideas can rise to the surface. Treating idea generation as a measurable, trainable practice — not a random occurrence — is what separates high-performing innovators from the rest.
original_quote: the proactive practice of exercising creative muscles so that the very best ideas can rise to the surface is vital for all to focus on these days
implication: Leaders and builders should treat creativity as a muscle with a measurable output (ideaflow), not as an unpredictable gift. If you can measure idea volume and quality, you can systematically improve it.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: Cross-pollination is a core strategy in Jeremy's framework for generating great ideas. Exposing yourself to diverse fields, disciplines, and contexts creates the raw material for novel combinations. This is not accidental — it must be pursued deliberately.
original_quote: we go deep into the concept of cross-pollination and cover several other strategies to discover as we discuss the 'dumb things' that geniuses just so happen to do to create great ideas and companies
implication: Creators and innovators should actively seek inputs from outside their domain. The most breakthrough ideas often come from connecting dots across unrelated fields, not from going deeper into a single silo.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: Jeremy frames certain unconventional or seemingly 'dumb' behaviors as the hidden practices of geniuses and great company builders. These counterintuitive habits are what actually produce breakthrough ideas. The implication is that the path to innovation often looks unserious or inefficient from the outside.
original_quote: the 'dumb things' that geniuses just so happen to do to create great ideas and companies
implication: Don't dismiss creative practices that seem silly or unproductive by conventional standards. The habits that look 'dumb' on the surface may be exactly what unlocks original thinking at the highest level.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: Jeremy's career path was not planned — it emerged through exposure to unexpected environments. A chance comment from a colleague in India ('you're kind of d-schooley') redirected him from management consulting toward design thinking and teaching. His entire career pivot happened because he kept poking his head into rooms that weren't his job.
original_quote: at some point, one of them said to me, you know, you're kind of de Schooley. And I took that as an insult... he goes, No, no, no, that's a that's a good thing.
implication: Career breakthroughs often come from following curiosity into adjacent spaces rather than executing a pre-set plan. Being open to informal signals and unexpected communities can be more valuable than rigid career roadmaps.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: Jeremy discovered the d.school not through formal research but by noticing interesting energy down the hall at a startup in India. Despite being enrolled at Stanford — where the d.school was founded — he had no awareness of it until outsiders pointed him toward it. The best opportunities are often invisible until someone names them for you.
original_quote: I keep hearing these interesting rumblings down the hall from the design department... And I said, What's that? They said, We're at Stanford, what do you mean? What's that? I said, I don't know.
implication: Proximity to great resources doesn't guarantee awareness of them. Leaders and educators should actively surface opportunities for people — especially the ones hiding in plain sight.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: The Stanford d.school was built on the insight that society's hardest problems don't fit neatly into a single discipline. David Kelley and co-founders created a space for cross-departmental collaboration — bringing MBAs, lawyers, doctors, engineers, and journalists together — because real-world challenges require teams with interdisciplinary fluency, not just deep domain expertise.
original_quote: a lot of the problems facing society don't fit neatly. They're just MBA problems, or just law problems there. They have lots of different facets to them. And we need to be equipping teams that are going to be interdisciplinary in nature, with language to bring their respective disciplinary expertise to bear.
implication: Organizations tackling complex problems should deliberately build interdisciplinary teams and invest in shared language for collaboration. Domain depth is necessary but not sufficient — you need the connective tissue of shared creative process.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: Jeremy's path to Stanford's d.school ran through direct exposure to poverty alleviation work — a venture accelerator in Bolivia, an AIDS orphanage in Zambia, and a solar startup in India. His deep curiosity about economic development, not a design background, was what made him 'd-schooley.' Passion for real human problems is a stronger predictor of design thinking aptitude than formal creative training.
original_quote: I got roped into a venture accelerator program in Bolivia. And I saw firsthand in the developing world, the power of entrepreneurship and small business to drive dignity and poverty alleviation.
implication: Design thinking and innovation education are most powerful when anchored to genuine human stakes. Recruiting for curiosity and empathy — not just creative credentials — builds stronger innovation teams.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: Jeremy spent 13+ years at the d.school and describes himself as 'just as invigorated' as when he started. This sustained engagement suggests that working at the intersection of creativity, teaching, and real-world problem solving creates durable intrinsic motivation — the kind that outlasts novelty.
original_quote: 13 years later, I'm just as invigorated by it, just as excited about it as I was then.
implication: When you find work that combines genuine curiosity, human impact, and continuous learning, engagement doesn't decay over time. For leaders building teams, creating those conditions matters more than extrinsic incentives.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: The d.school was explicitly structured to tear down university silos. Rather than granting degrees or admitting its own students, it hosted cross-departmental classes where disciplines collided. This structural choice — no territorial degree program — was what made genuine interdisciplinary collaboration possible.
original_quote: especially when I joined, we didn't grant degrees, we didn't admit students. And so what we did is, we would host kind of cross departmental collaborative opportunities.
implication: Institutions and organizations that want to foster innovation should consider whether their structural incentives (degrees, departments, job titles) are creating silos that prevent the cross-pollination they claim to want. Sometimes the most innovative move is to not own the output.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: Jeremy grew up the son of a preacher, with limited exposure to professional career models. His childhood vision of success was 'people wearing ties carrying boxes with handles' — essentially, briefcase-carrying professionals. This origin story illustrates how early environmental exposure shapes creative horizons and career imagination.
original_quote: in my mind, there's, you know, there's people who do makeup at Dillards. And there's preachers and there's working the drive thru. I think I want to do that thing where like, I see people, they look very important. They're wearing ties, and they're carrying boxes with the handles on them.
implication: Expanding what people can imagine for themselves — through exposure to diverse role models and environments — is a prerequisite for unlocking creative and professional potential. This applies to education, mentorship, and organizational culture.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show
